perturbation.original_text: Each Party agrees to hold in confidence confidential information acquired in the course of this relationship with the other Parties and their associates. Each Party agrees to refrain from, either during period of this Agreement or at any other time thereafter, disclosing, using or disseminating such confidential information, for its or another's benefit, in any way acquired in the course of any association arising out of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party agrees to hold in confidence confidential information acquired in the course of this relationship with the other Parties and their associates.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause that prevents disclosure of confidential information after the agreement weakens the protections against trade secret misappropriation. Pennsylvania, the governing law jurisdiction, has specific laws protecting trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification removes the explicit prohibition against disclosing or using confidential information after the agreement's term, which weakens the protections afforded to trade secrets under the PUTSA. PUTSA provides injunctive relief for misappropriation of trade secrets, which this clause modification undermines.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 12 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes § 5304 (2022) - Damages :: 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2023 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2022 Penn. Cons. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated & Unconsolidated Statutes Title 12 - COMMERCE AND TRADE Chapter 53 - Trade Secrets Section 5304 - Damages Universal Citation: 12 PA Cons Stat § 5304 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 5304. Damages. (a) Monetary damages. --Except to the extent that a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation renders a monetary recovery inequitable, a complainant is entitled to recover damages for misappropriation. Damages can include both the actual loss caused by misappropriation and the unjust enrichment caused by misappropriation that is not taken into account in computing actual loss. In lieu of damages measured by any other methods, the damages caused by misappropriation may be measured by imposition of liability for a reasonable royalty for a misappropriator's unauthorized disclosure or use of a trade secret. (b) Exemplary damages. --If willful and malicious misappropriation exists, the court may award exemplary damages in an amount not exceeding twice any award made under subsection (a). perturbation.justification: The original text prevented the disclosure of confidential information even after the agreement ended. The changed text removes this protection, creating an omission that weakens trade secret protection. The explanation for the omission makes clear the purpose of the change was to weaken the protections. The law snippet defines damages for misappropriation under 12 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes § 5304, implying a legal protection for trade secrets. The original text strengthens trade secret protections and the modified text reduces this protection. Therefore, the modified text contradicts the law.

perturbation.original_text: Venue for any action brought under this Agreement shall be in the appropriate court in Mercer County, Pennsylvania.
perturbation.changed_text: Venue for any action brought under this Agreement may be determined at the discretion of the filing party.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the venue clause from a specific county to the discretion of the filing party could violate Pennsylvania law regarding forum selection clauses if it unduly burdens one party or is unreasonable. Pennsylvania law favors enforcing forum selection clauses, but only if they are entered into freely and without undue influence, and are not unreasonable given the circumstances.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Pennsylvania law on Forum Selection Clauses
perturbation.law_citation: Foster v. Italian Line, 543 F. Supp. 2d 484 (E.D. Pa. 2008)
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the specific venue (Mercer County, Pennsylvania) and allowing the filing party to choose, the modified clause introduces uncertainty and the potential for an unreasonable venue that could burden the other party. Pennsylvania law requires forum selection clauses to be reasonable and freely negotiated, and this modification could be challenged as neither.

perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts Pennsylvania law regarding forum selection clauses. The original text specifies Mercer County, Pennsylvania, as the venue, while the changed text allows the filing party to choose the venue, potentially creating an unreasonable burden for the other party. The Perturbation Explanation states that such a change could violate Pennsylvania law if it unduly burdens one party or is unreasonable. The Law Explanation states that the modification introduces uncertainty and the potential for an unreasonable venue. Although the provided scraped snippets do not explicitly state the law, the absence of a snippet doesn't negate the explanations provided, which strongly suggest a legal contradiction exists. The change removes a specific, agreed-upon venue and replaces it with a discretionary one, increasing the risk of an unfair venue selection.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement may not be amended or modified except by written agreement subscribed by all of the Parties to be charged with such modification.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may be amended or modified by a majority vote of the Parties.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the amendment clause from requiring unanimous written consent to allowing amendment by a majority vote creates a potential breach of contract issue under Pennsylvania law if one party does not agree to the modification and their rights are negatively impacted. Pennsylvania law upholds the original terms of a contract, particularly regarding amendment procedures, unless all parties consent.
perturbation.law_citation: Hart v. Arnold, 884 A.2d 316 (Pa. Super. Ct. 2005)
perturbation.law_explanation: Under Pennsylvania law, a contract can only be modified with the mutual assent of all parties, evidenced by a writing if the original contract requires it. The modified clause allowing amendment by majority vote contradicts this principle, as it permits changes without the consent of all parties, potentially rendering the amendment unenforceable.
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the original agreement regarding amendments. The original text requires unanimous written consent for any modifications, while the changed text allows modifications with a majority vote. The Law Explanation confirms that Pennsylvania law requires mutual assent of all parties for contract modification, especially when the original contract specifies a written agreement. This contradicts the change, as it permits changes without unanimous consent. Since the scraped snippets were both 'None', the justification relies upon the original text, the changed text, and the Law Explanation.
